claimText: Solar flares are classified into A, B, C, M, and X classes, with A being the smallest and X being the largest.
evidenceExcerpt: Solar flares are classified from smallest to largest as A, B, C, M, and X-class flares.
examUse: Statement-based question on the classification and intensity scale of solar flares.
distractorHooks: Reversing the order to claim X is the smallest and A is the largest
Using a different classification scale such as Alpha, Beta, Gamma

claimText: The frequency of solar flares peaks during the solar maximum, which is the height of the Sun's 11-year magnetic cycle.
evidenceExcerpt: Solar flares occur most frequently during the solar maximum, which is the peak of the Sun's 11-year magnetic cycle.
examUse: Statement-based question linking solar flare frequency to the 11-year solar cycle.
distractorHooks: Claiming solar flares are most frequent during the solar minimum
Stating the solar cycle is an annual or 100-year cycle instead of 11 years

claimText: Coronal Mass Ejections (CMEs) are large expulsions of plasma and magnetic fields from the Sun's corona.
evidenceExcerpt: Coronal Mass Ejections (CMEs) are large expulsions of plasma and magnetic fields from the Sun's corona.
examUse: Definition or correct-pair question distinguishing CMEs from regular solar flares or solar winds.
distractorHooks: Claiming CMEs originate from the Sun's core rather than the corona
Describing CMEs as purely light and radiation rather than plasma and magnetic fields

claimText: The Chandrayaan-2 Lunar Orbiter, through its CHACE-2 payload, made the first-ever observation of the effects of a Coronal Mass Ejection on the Moon.
evidenceExcerpt: Chandrayaan-2's CHACE-2 made the first-ever observation of the effects of a Coronal Mass Ejection on the Moon.
examUse: Current affairs or mission-specific question regarding the achievements of the Chandrayaan-2 mission.
distractorHooks: Attributing the observation to Chandrayaan-3 or a NASA mission like the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter
Claiming the observation was made by a rover rather than an orbiter payload
